learning_objectives:
Understand how to direct program execution
using decision-making statements.

Master the syntax and usage of traditional
and modern switch expressions.

Implement repetitive tasks using various loop
structures.
Control loop behavior using break, continue, and labels.
Choose the correct control flow structure for specific logic requirements.
prerequisites: Understanding of Java basic data types and variables.
Knowledge of relational and logical operators (==, !=, >, <, &&, ||).
introduction:
By default, a Java program runs from top to bottom. Control
flow statements allow you to break this linear path. You can jump over code,
choose between different paths, or repeat a block of code multiple times. This
is where logic enters your programming.

concept_explanation.decision_making:
Decision-making statements (if-else and switch) allow your
program to execute specific blocks of code based on a condition. An 'if'
statement evaluates a boolean expression. A 'switch' statement compares a single
value against multiple possible constants.
concept_explanation.iteration:
Iteration statements
(loops) repeat a block of code. 'for' loops are best when you know the number of
repetitions. 'while' loops are best when repeating until a condition changes.
'do-while' guarantees the code runs at least once.
concept_explanation.branching:
Branching
statements (break, continue) alter the loop's path. 'break' exits the loop
entirely, while 'continue' skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to
the next one.

syntax_breakdown.if_statement:
The 'if' keyword is followed by a boolean condition in
parentheses. If true, the curly braces block executes.
syntax_breakdown.switch_expression:
Unlike the old switch statement, the switch expression uses the arrow (->)
syntax, doesn't require break statements to prevent fall-through, and can return
a value.
syntax_breakdown.labeled_break:
A label is an identifier followed by a colon
(labelName:). A 'break labelName;' statement exits the specific loop marked by
that label, which is useful for nested loops.

rules_and_key_points:
An
'if' condition must always result in a boolean (true/false).

The 'else' block
is optional and executes only if all preceding conditions are false.

Traditional switch statements require a 'break' to prevent 'fall-through'
(executing subsequent cases).

Switch expressions using '->' do not have
fall-through and are generally safer.

The 'for' loop initialization happens
once; the condition is checked before every iteration; the update happens after
every iteration.

The 'do-while' loop checks the condition after the block
executes, ensuring at least one run.

The enhanced for loop (for-each) is
read-only regarding the collection structure; you cannot modify the collection
size during iteration.

A 'continue' statement stops the current iteration and
jumps to the update/condition check of the loop.

Infinite loops occur if the
loop condition never becomes false; avoid these by ensuring a clear exit path.

Variables declared inside a loop or if-block are local to that block and cannot
be accessed outside.

code_example_1.title:
Basic Grade Checker
(if-else and traditional switch)
code_example_1.description:
This program demonstrates
simple branching logic using if-else and a traditional switch statement.

code_example_2.title: Modern Switch and Loop Control
code_example_2.description:
Demonstrating Java 21
Switch Expressions and Labeled Breaks in nested loops.

code.output:
WEDNESDAY is a Weekday
i: 1, j: 1
i: 1,
j: 2
i: 1, j: 3
i: 2, j: 1
Breaking outer loop at i=2, j=2

code.step_by_step_dry_run:
In the nested loop example: 1. i=1 starts. Inner loop
runs for j=1, 2, 3. Prints all. 2. i increments to 2. 3. Inner loop starts. j=1
prints. 4. Inner loop hits j=2. The condition (i==2 && j==2) is now TRUE. 5.
'break outerLoop' is triggered. Instead of just exiting the 'j' loop, it
terminates the 'i' loop entirely. Program continues after the outerLoop block.
code.memory_visualization:
When a loop runs: 1. Stack Frame: The loop control
variable (like 'int i') is created on the Stack. 2. Scope: As soon as the loop
finishes, 'i' is popped off the stack and is no longer accessible. 3. Heap: If
the loop creates objects, they stay in the Heap until Garbage Collection, but
the references in the Stack are removed when the block closes.
real_world_use_cases:
Menu Navigation: Using switch statements to handle
user menu selections.

Data Processing: Using for-each loops to iterate
through a list of customer transactions.

Validation: Using while loops to
repeatedly ask for a password until it meets security criteria.

Game Loops:
Using a 'while(running)' loop to keep a game engine active until the player
quits.
common_mistakes:
Using '=' instead of '==' in an if condition
(though Java prevents this for non-boolean types, it's common in other
languages).

Forgetting the 'break' in traditional switch statements, leading
to fall-through.

Placing a semicolon after the if condition: if(x > 5); { ...
} - this makes the block execute regardless of the condition.

Infinite loops
caused by failing to update the loop counter (e.g., forgetting i++).

Off-by-one errors: Using '<=' when '<' was intended, causing the loop to run
one extra time.
best_practices:
Prefer Switch Expressions (->) over
traditional switch statements for cleaner, safer code.

Always use curly
braces {} for if/else and loops, even if there is only one line of code.

Keep
loop bodies small; if a loop is too long, move the logic into a separate
method.

Use the enhanced for loop (for-each) whenever you don't need the
index of the elements.

Avoid deep nesting (e.g., an 'if' inside a 'for'
inside an 'if'); try to use 'return' or 'continue' to 'fail fast' and flatten
the code.

frequently_asked_questions.question:
What is the
difference between break and continue?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
'break' stops the loop
immediately. 'continue' stops only the current iteration and jumps to the next
cycle of the loop.

frequently_asked_questions.question:
When should I use a while loop vs a for
loop?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
Use a 'for' loop when you know exactly how many times to
repeat (e.g., 1 to 10). Use a 'while' loop when the exit condition depends on a
logic state (e.g., until a file is fully read).

code.summary:
Control flow
is the heart of logic in Java. By mastering 'if-else' for branching, 'switch'
for multiple fixed options, and 'loops' for repetition, you can build programs
that react to data and handle complex tasks efficiently. Modern Java (21)
provides enhanced switch features that make this logic even more robust.
key_takeaways:
Switch
expressions are the modern way to handle multi-way branches.

For loops are
for fixed iterations.
While loops are for conditional iterations.

Enhanced for loops simplify array/collection
traversal.
Break exits loops; Continue skips iterations.

Labels allow
control over nested loops.

Scope limits variable visibility to the block
where they are defined.

Logic should be readable and avoid excessive
nesting.
code.preview_of_next_topic:
In Phase 4, we will dive into 'Arrays',
where you will learn how to store and manage multiple values in a single
variable and how to use the loops we learned today to process that data.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a 'while' loop and a 'do-while' loop?
questions.answer:
The primary difference is the timing of the condition check. A
'while' loop checks the condition before executing the block, meaning it might
never run if the condition is initially false. A 'do-while' loop executes the
block first and then checks the condition, guaranteeing that the code inside
runs at least once.

questions.question:
In a switch expression (Java 14+), what does the arrow (->) replace
compared to the traditional switch?
questions.answer:
The arrow (->) replaces both
the colon (:) and the 'break' statement. It indicates that if the case matches,
only the expression or block to the right of the arrow will execute, and then
the switch will immediately terminate without falling through to other cases.

questions.question:
Write a code snippet that uses an enhanced for loop to print all elements of an
integer array named 'numbers'.

Create a
program that prints numbers from 1 to 20, but skips multiples of 3 using the
'continue' keyword and stops completely if the number hits 17 using 'break'.

What is wrong with this code: 'if (x
= 10) { ... }' assuming x is an int?
questions.answer:
The error is using the
assignment operator (=) instead of the equality operator (==). In Java, an 'if'
condition must be a boolean. 'x = 10' returns 10 (an int), not a boolean, so the
code will fail to compile. The correct version is 'if (x == 10)'.

questions.question:
Explain the danger
of 'fall-through' in a traditional switch statement and how to prevent it.
questions.answer:
Fall-through occurs when a case in a traditional switch statement
does not end with a 'break'. Java will continue executing the code for all
subsequent cases regardless of whether they match the variable, until it hits a
'break' or the end of the switch. This often leads to logical bugs. It is
prevented by adding 'break;' at the end of every case or by using the modern
switch expression (->).

questions.question: Write a nested loop that prints a 3x3 grid of asterisks ().

code.question:
Using a labeled break,
write a program that searches for the number 5 in a 2D array and stops all loops
as soon as it's found.

mcqs.question:
Which loop is guaranteed to execute at least
once?
mcqs.explanation:
The do-while loop
evaluates its condition after the code block has executed.

mcqs.question:
What happens if a traditional switch case does not have a break
statement?

options.C: It falls through to the next case

mcqs.explanation:
This behavior is known as
fall-through, where execution continues into subsequent cases.

mcqs.question:
What is the output of: for(int i=0; i<3; i++) { if(i==1) continue;
System.out.print(i); }
mcqs.explanation:
When i is 1, the continue statement skips
the print and moves to the next iteration (i=2).

mcqs.question:
Which keyword is used in a switch expression to return a value from a block of
code?
mcqs.explanation:
In Java switch expressions, 'yield' is used to
return a value when a multi-line block is used in a case.

Both 'if'
conditions are independent and both evaluate to true.

mcqs.question: Identify the bug: for(int i=0; i<10; i--); { System.out.print(i); }
mcqs.explanation:
The i-- makes the condition i<10 always true (infinite
loop), and the semicolon makes the for loop body empty.

code_example_2.explanation:
In Java, the while condition must
be a boolean 'true' for an infinite loop.

A simple 'break' exits
the innermost loop currently executing.

code_example_2.question:
Which
approach is best for iterating through an array without needing the index?
code_example_2.explanation:
The enhanced for
loop (for-each) is specifically designed for clean, index-free iteration.
